title: Pita bread
subtitle: Middle Eastern flatbreads that puff in a very hot oven
excerpt: Yeasted flatbreads baked in a very hot oven, where they puff dramatically to create a hollow pocket. The whole process takes about two hours. Makes eight pitas.
sourceNotes: Pita bread is documented across the Levant, Egypt, and the broader Middle East from antiquity. The pocket forms when steam forces the two layers of dough apart during baking in a very hot oven, a property documented in Middle Eastern and Mediterranean household baking records. The form recorded here follows the standard household pita as documented in twentieth-century Middle Eastern household baking literature, baked directly on a hot oven floor or baking stone.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze fully cooled in a sealed bag. Warm directly from frozen in a dry frying pan for 2 minutes per side.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly. Bake in batches of four on a hot baking tray.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be refrigerated after the bulk ferment for up to 24 hours. Bring to room temperature, shape, and bake.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: maximum fan oven temperature; highest setting in a conventional oven. Preheat the baking tray for 15 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: The process by which pita bread forms an internal hollow. When the thin round of dough hits the extreme heat of a very hot oven surface, steam builds between the two layers of dough faster than the outer surface can set, forcing them apart. The result is a balloon that collapses as it cools into a flatbread with a pocket. For this to work, the oven and tray must be preheated fully, and the dough must be rolled evenly — thick patches prevent even puffing.

content.text: The pita's pocket forms in a blast of heat. The oven must be as hot as it will go and the baking tray must be preheated with it. When the pita goes onto the hot surface, the extreme heat causes rapid steam production inside the dough — this is the
moment. A lukewarm tray will produce flat, unpocketed pita. They still taste the same, but the pocket is the reason to make them at home.
Make the dough and prove — 60 minutes

Combine the {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{yeast-dried}} in a large bowl. Add 250 ml warm water and {{olive-oil}} and mix to a smooth dough. Knead for 8–10 minutes, until soft and elastic. Cover and leave at room temperature for 60 minutes, until doubled.
Shape and bake — 3–4 minutes per batch

Place a baking tray on the top shelf of the oven and preheat to 250°C fan (maximum temperature). Leave the tray in the oven for at least 15 minutes. Divide the dough into eight equal pieces, about 80 g each. Roll each piece on a lightly floured surface into an oval about 5 mm thick and 18 cm long. Leave to rest for 15–20 minutes.

Working quickly, slide two or three pitas onto the hot tray. Bake for 3–4 minutes until puffed and just beginning to colour. They will deflate as they cool. Wrap in a clean tea towel to keep them soft. Repeat with the remaining pitas.
